Nine separate blazes on the lee side of the Big Island of Hawaii kept firefighters busy in the final days of October 2007.  The fires consumed approximately 2500 acres of brush from northwest of Kawaihae to southwest of Waikoloa.  County officials believed the fires were deliberately set.  An estimated 400 residents of Puako had to be evacuated on Sunday, October 28th, because of thick smoke in the area.  They were allowed to return to their homes Sunday evening.  Culturally sensitive archaeological sites near Puako complicated the firefighting efforts to some extent.  Instead of using bulldozers to battle the fire in that area, they used saws and other hand tools to prevent possible damage to the cultural sites.  The fires were contained by the 31st.  There were no reports of serious injuries or property damage.

Conditions remained dry in Upcountry Maui, where a mandatory 10 percent reduction in water consumption by all non-agricultural customers remained in effect.  No crop damage estimates were available.||This part of Maui County was put under the D2 classification in the Drought Monitor as of June 12th, 2007.

A 30 percent reduction in water consumption for users of the Waimanalo Irrigation System remained in effect.  Dry conditions continued to persist in this portion of Oahu.||This part of Honolulu County was put into the D2 classification in the Drought Monitor as of September 6th, 2007.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain fell over the summits of Mauna Kea and Mauna Loa on the Big Island of Hawaii.  The road to the summits was closed for a time on the 17th of February until the ice on the road surfaces melted.

Heavy showers over Lanai caused small stream and drainage ditch flooding, and ponding on roadways.  There were no reports of serious property damage or injuries.

A fire blackened 300 acres of brush in West Oahu.  The blaze was near Kamaile Elementary School in Waianae, but winds were favorable and blew the fire away from the school and other structures.  There were no reports of serious injuries or property damage.  The cause of the fire was being investigated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fire scorched 1500 acres of mainly brush in an area 6 miles south/southeast of Waikoloa on the west side of the Big Island of Hawaii.   Mamalahoa Highway was closed for a few hours by the fire, as was one lane of Waikoloa Road, south of Kamuela.  Traffic became snarled under the circumstances.  The cause of the blaze was unknown.  No homes were evacuated, and no structures were threatened.  There were no significant injuries.|

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven people had to be evacuated when a brush fire moved closed to their homes about two-and-a-half miles east/southeast of Lahaina in West Maui.  The fire eventually blackened 1350 acres, and destroyed one small structure and several vehicles.  Damage estimates were not available.  There were no significant injuries.  The cause of the fire was under investigation, though fire officials believed it was intentionally set.|

A fire burned more than 3000 acres of dry brush about 2.5 miles northeast  of Waikoloa Village in the South Kohala District on the Big Island of Hawaii.  The blaze threatened no structures as it burned Friday and Saturday, 27 and 28 July.  The fire began at approximately 1:45 PM on the 27th, though the cause was unknown.  There were no reports of serious property damage or injuries.

A brush fire in West Oahu, fueled by dry vegetation, blackened 35 acres of kiawe and grass. The blaze got to within 150 feet of the Waianae Coast Comprehensive Health Center, where 200 patients and employees left as a precaution.  Residents from six homes in Maili also evacuated as the fire neared.  However, there were no serious injuries or property damage.  The cause of the fire was unknown.

Lingering instability and moisture from the recent Kona storm caused heavy showers over the Kau District on the Big Island of Hawaii.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy showers resulted in flash flooding conditions in the Kawa Flats area in the Kau District on the Big Island of Hawaii.  Hawaii Belt Road, also know as Highway 11, was closed for a time because of the deep water flowing across the road surface.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm brought heavy rains, thunderstorms, high winds, and blizzard conditions to Hawaii in the early part of December.  A strong low pressure system northwest of Kauai, along with a cold front moving through the islands, caused significant damage across the state.  One fatality can be attributed to the storm, and two known injuries.  Trees were toppled, along with power poles and lines.  Flash flooding damaged homes, and the inclement weather snarled traffic.  The state estimates total damages to be about $5 million.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees were blown over along the Kona coast due to wind gusts.  Some trees fell across transmission lines causing power outages.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds and wind gusts, frequently over 35 mph, caused blowing snow and poor visibilities on the summits of Mauna Kea and Mauna Loa.  Sustained winds reached 76 mph on Mauna Kea around 1400HST on December 5th.  A hiker, on the afternoon of the 5th, left his car at the Hale Pohaku parking lot on Mauna Kea and apparently ventured off.  Firefighters and police special response team members searched for the man, but he was never found.  The man was presumed dead.  A road crew reported high winds, blowing snow, and near zero visibility on the morning of the 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire in a remote area between the 6000- and 7000-foot elevations on the south- and southwest-facing slopes of Haleakala in East Maui scorched about 1700 acres of mainly brush, though some native plants were damaged and destroyed.  The fire was sustained by extremely dry conditions, low humidity, and gusty south to southwest winds.  Damage estimates were not available.  There were no serious injuries.  The cause of the blaze was under investigation, but was believed started by an illegal campfire at the upper Waiohuli trail in the Polipoli Springs State Recreational Area.

Gusty south winds, low humidity, and dry vegetation were the ingredients necessary to sustain a wildfire that consumed about 2700 acres of brush in leeward Kohala on the Big Island.  The fire burned a 3-mile swath along Highway 190 - Mamalahoa Highway - near its intersection with Waikoloa Road, around 10 miles south/southwest of Kamuela.  Damage estimates were not available.  There were no serious injuries.  Downed electrical wires may have started the blaze.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds in Honolulu County resulted in three serious injuries.  A 38-year-old woman at a Sheridan Street address ended up losing parts of two fingers after a door slammed on her hand when gusty winds swept through her home.  She was taken to The Queen's Medical Center in serious condition.  |In another incident, two individuals were struck by an airborne beach chair at the Royal Hawaiian Hotel and were hospitalized in stable condition at Straub Hospital.

Dry conditions persisted in Upcountry Maui, and a mandatory 10 percent reduction in water consumption by all non-agricultural customers remained in effect.  No crop damage estimates were available.||This portion of Maui County was put into the D2 classification in the Drought Monitor as of June 12th.

A fire burned approximately 9100 acres of dry pasture land in the leeward section of the Big Island of Hawaii, about 2 miles south/southeast of Waikoloa Village.  The blaze forced the closure of Mamalahoa Highway and Waikoloa Road for a time as crews worked to contain the fire's spread.  There were no reports of property damage or injuries.  Fire officials believed the fire was intentionally set.

Rain bands from Tropical Depression Cosme caused small stream and drainage ditch flooding, and ponding on roadways in portions of the Hilo, Puna, and Kau districts on the windward side of the Big Island of Hawaii.  There were no reports of serious injuries or property damage.

A fire 2.5 miles northeast of Maunaloa in the western part of Molokai scorched 3000 acres of brush.  Dry conditions and winds of 10 to 20 mph helped sustain the blaze. No homes or other structures were threatened, and no significant injuries were reported.  Damage estimates were not available.  The cause of the fire was under investigation.

A fire blackened 600 acres of mainly brush 7 miles east of Waikoloa Village on the lee side of the Big Island of Hawaii.  Dry conditions in the area, along with winds occasionally gusting to 20 mph, were factors in the spread of the fire.  No structures became threatened, and no serious injuries were reported.  Damage estimates were not available.  The cause of the blaze was unknown.

A large fire consumed 2600 acres of brush above Olowalu in West Maui, about 4 miles south/southeast of Lahaina.  Very dry conditions and winds occasionally gusting between 35 and 50 mph prevented the fire from being quickly brought under control.||The blaze destroyed two homes and damaged two others, and caused the evacuation of several other residences.  The fire also wiped out a hydroponic tomato operation, at a loss of $300 thousand.  Power poles and electric transmission lines were downed by the wildfire, as well. ||More than 400 people, mainly tourists, had to find shelter at a Red Cross site because the fire prevented many individuals from getting to, or from, the airport in Kahului.  The blaze forced road closures on the 27th and 28th of June, which in turn produced massive traffic jams, particularly on Honoapiilani Highway.||A total for all the damages was not available.  No serious injuries were reported.  The cause of the blaze was unknown.

Dry conditions became severe in portions of leeward Kohala and leeward Hamakua on the Big Island of Hawaii.  County officials issued a mandatory   25 percent reduction in water use for several communities.  Crop damage estimates were not available.||Areas of the Hamakua and Kohala districts were placed into the D2 classification in the Drought Monitior as of June 12th.

Dry conditions became severe in portions of leeward Kohala and leeward Hamakua on the Big Island of Hawaii.  County officials issued a mandatory   25 percent reduction in water use for several communities.  Crop damage estimates were not available.||Areas of the Hamakua and Kohala districts were placed into the D2 classification in the Drought Monitior as of June 12th.

A blaze scorched about 7000 acres of dry brush in the North Shore area of Oahu, near Waialua.  The fire raged from Sunday, August 12, through Sunday, August 19.  It began at 1:40 P.M. on the 12th near the University of Hawaii Experimental Farm near Kaukonahua Road and spread in all directions over the next week.  Residents close to the fire were evacuated for a time as crews worked to contain the blaze, and many homes became covered with soot.  However, in the end, there were no serious injuries or significant property damage.  The cause of the fire was not determined.

Drought conditions eased in leeward Kohala and leeward Hamakua in early August due to increased rainfall and completion of backup water source projects in the two districts.  The Water Restriction Notice that had been issued by Hawaii County was downgraded to a Water Conservation Notice as of 10 August.||This part of the Big Island of Hawaii had been placed in the D2 classification of the Drought Monitor as of June 12th, 2007.

A kona low initially developed north of Hawaii and generated showers, thunderstorms, and drenching rain across much of the state.  The system caused flash flooding in a couple of instances.  Damage estimates were not available. There were no reports of serious injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding conditions in the southern part of Molokai.  Kamehameha V Highway near Kawela was closed for a time by water and two feet of mud that had overflowed the roadway from the nearby Kawela Gulch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The kona low that had earlier affected much of the state remained strong enough to generate heavy rain over the southeast portion of the Big Island  of Hawaii.  No significant property damage or injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy showers in the Kau District of the Big Island of Hawaii, in the Kawa Flats area between Pahala and Naalehu, caused flash flooding along the Hawaii Belt Road.  Officials reported about two feet of water covering the roadway at one point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough moving from the northwest to southeast over the Hawaiian Islands helped generate heavy rain, thunderstorms, and showers.  Some of the rainfall caused flash flooding on the island of Kauai and on the Big Island of Hawaii.  Damage estimates were not available.  There were no serious injuries reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy showers and thunderstorms caused flash flooding in Lihue on the Garden Isle of Kauai.  Water rose to about one foot on Nawiliwili Road near the Borders bookstore, as it did at the intersection of Ahukini Road and Akaha Street.  There were no reports of serious injuries.  Damage reports were not available.

An upper low over the Hawaiian Islands induced showers and isolated thunderstorms toward the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused the Hanalei River to overflow and flood the roadway across the Hanalei Bridge.  That portion of Kuhio Highway was closed until the water subsided.  There were no reports of significant property damage or injuries.|

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough and a shear line at the surface combined to produce heavy showers over the islands, with Oahu and Molokai seeing the most significant events.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding conditions closed Kamehameha Highway near Waikane for a time.
